description: Minimalist e-commerce platform in Spring Boot. Serves as a didactic example of Harness Engineering with a multi-agent architecture.

features.name: product_catalog
features.title: Product Catalog API
features.description: Expose a read-only product catalog through a RESTful API following standard 3-layer architecture. The service layer is the only component allowed to access the repository; the controller must never interact with JPA directly.
technical_constraints: Java 17+, Spring Boot 3.x, Spring Data JPA
H2 in-memory database for tests; schema auto-created via ddl-auto=create-drop
DTOs must be Java Records — no mutable POJOs for response objects
MapStruct or manual mapping permitted; ModelMapper is not allowed
No business logic in controller or repository layers
acceptance: Product entity is annotated with @Entity and @Table; all fields are mapped with explicit @Column names — no implicit naming
ProductRepository extends JpaRepository<Product, Long> with no custom queries needed for this feature
ProductDTO is a Java Record with fields: Long id, String name, String description, BigDecimal price, int stock
GET /api/products returns HTTP 200 with a JSON array; returns an empty array (not null) when no products exist
GET /api/products/{id} returns HTTP 200 with a single ProductDTO when the product exists
GET /api/products/{id} returns HTTP 404 with a structured JSON error body (not an empty response) when the product does not exist
ProductService.findAll() and findById() are annotated with @Transactional(readOnly = true)
ResourceNotFoundException is a custom unchecked exception; it must NOT extend RuntimeException directly — it must be domain-scoped (e.g. extend a base DomainException)
ProductServiceTest covers: findAll returns mapped DTOs, findById returns correct DTO, findById throws ResourceNotFoundException for missing id — Mockito only, no Spring context loaded
ProductControllerIntegrationTest uses @SpringBootTest + MockMvc + H2; verifies HTTP status, Content-Type, JSON field values, and that the empty-list endpoint returns [] not null
No System.out.println or e.printStackTrace() anywhere in production code
pitfalls: Returning null instead of an empty list from findAll()
Swallowing the ResourceNotFoundException in a try-catch and returning null
Leaking JPA entities directly from the controller response
@Transactional on the repository instead of the service

features.name: order_checkout
features.title: Order Creation and Stock Validation
features.description: Allow customers to create an order containing one or more products. The operation must be fully atomic: stock is deducted and the order is persisted in the same transaction. Any failure must roll back the entire operation, leaving no partial state in the database.
technical_constraints: Stock deduction and order persistence must occur within a single @Transactional method — no saveAndFlush workarounds
InsufficientStockException must trigger transaction rollback — verify it is unchecked or explicitly declared in rollbackFor
CreateOrderRequest must be validated with Jakarta Bean Validation (@NotEmpty, @Min) — invalid requests must return HTTP 400 before reaching the service
Error responses must follow a uniform structure: { timestamp, status, error, message, path }
Order total must be calculated server-side from current product prices — never trusted from the request body
acceptance: Order entity has: Long id, List<OrderItem> items, BigDecimal total, LocalDateTime createdAt, OrderStatus status (enum: PENDING, CONFIRMED, CANCELLED)
OrderItem is a @Embeddable or separate @Entity with productId, quantity, and unitPrice captured at time of order
POST /api/orders accepts CreateOrderRequest { List<OrderItemRequest> items } where each item has productId (Long, @NotNull) and quantity (int, @Min(1))
POST /api/orders returns HTTP 201 with OrderDTO on success; the response includes the generated order id and computed total
If any product in the order does not exist, the endpoint returns HTTP 404 and no stock is modified
If any product has insufficient stock, InsufficientStockException is thrown, HTTP 422 is returned, and no stock is deducted for any item in the request
Stock deduction is validated before any write — the service must not partially deduct stock across items before detecting an insufficient-stock condition
GlobalExceptionHandler (@RestControllerAdvice) handles ResourceNotFoundException → 404, InsufficientStockException → 422, MethodArgumentNotValidException → 400 — all with the uniform error structure
OrderServiceTest verifies: successful order reduces stock for all items, exception is thrown without stock modification when stock is insufficient, exception is thrown when a product does not exist
Tests use @Transactional on the test method or manual rollback to avoid polluting other test cases
pitfalls: Deducting stock item-by-item inside a loop before checking all items — causes partial stock corruption on failure
Using checked exceptions without rollbackFor — Spring will commit the transaction even after the exception
Trusting the total from the request body instead of computing it from the database
Returning HTTP 500 instead of a structured 4xx for known domain errors
Not capturing unitPrice at order time — price changes will corrupt historical order totals

features.name: inventory_management
features.title: Backoffice Inventory Management
features.description: Provide admin endpoints to add new products to the catalog and update existing stock levels. All inputs must be validated before reaching the service layer. Endpoints must be idempotent where appropriate and must not expose internal identifiers or JPA internals in error messages.
technical_constraints: No authentication required for this feature — assume a trusted internal network (auth is out of scope)
Request DTOs must use Jakarta Bean Validation annotations; validation errors must return HTTP 400 with field-level detail
CreateProductRequest and UpdateStockRequest must be separate classes — do not reuse ProductDTO for input
Service methods that write data must be annotated with @Transactional (without readOnly)
Duplicate product names must be rejected at the service layer with a descriptive exception — not silently overwritten
acceptance: POST /api/products accepts CreateProductRequest { String name (@NotBlank), String description (@NotBlank), BigDecimal price (@NotNull, @DecimalMin("0.01")), int stock (@Min(0)) }
POST /api/products returns HTTP 201 with the full ProductDTO including the generated id; the Location header is set to /api/products/{id}
If a product with the same name already exists, POST /api/products returns HTTP 409 Conflict with a structured error body
PUT /api/products/{id}/stock accepts UpdateStockRequest { int quantity (@Min(0)) } — quantity is the new absolute stock value, not a delta
PUT /api/products/{id}/stock returns HTTP 200 with the updated ProductDTO
PUT /api/products/{id}/stock returns HTTP 404 if the product does not exist — the GlobalExceptionHandler must handle this, not a try-catch in the controller
Both endpoints reject requests with invalid fields and return HTTP 400 with a body listing which fields failed and why
Integration tests verify: HTTP 201 + Location header on creation, HTTP 409 on duplicate name, HTTP 200 + updated stock value on PUT, HTTP 400 on invalid input, HTTP 404 on unknown id for PUT
No integration test may rely on a hardcoded product id — ids must be obtained dynamically from a prior save or from the response body
ProductService has no overloaded method named 'save' used for both create and update — operations must be distinct methods with clear intent
pitfalls: Setting the Location header with a hardcoded base URL instead of using UriComponentsBuilder from the request context
Using the same DTO for both create input and HTTP response — exposes internal fields and breaks API contract evolution
Treating stock update as a delta (adding quantity) instead of an absolute set — undefined behavior on concurrent requests
Relying on a database unique constraint exception (DataIntegrityViolationException) instead of an explicit pre-check — produces a non-descriptive 500 or leaks SQL error messages
Hardcoding product ids in integration tests — tests become order-dependent and fail on clean runs
